food_name: Chicken Pongteh (Nonya Braised Chicken in Fermented Soybean Paste with Bamboo Shoots)
aliases: Nonya chicken braised in fermented soybean paste

english: Nonya chicken braised in fermented soybean paste

region_of_origin: Singapore and Malacca, Malaysia (Straits Chinese Peranakan)
food_subgroup: Slow-braised chicken in sweet-savoury fermented soybean paste sauce — chicken pieces (bone-in, skin-on) with bamboo shoots

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Peranakan braised chicken in fermented soybean paste. A0CKL (cooked chicken meat) used as closest defensible parent anchor.

serving.description: 1 serving = 420g total (200g chicken pieces + 80g sauce + 20g bamboo shoots + 120g steamed rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Chicken Pongteh is approximately 420g, comprising 200g bone-in chicken pieces with sauce, 20g bamboo shoots, and 120g steamed white rice, as typically plated at Nonya restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Pongteh — IMR Malaysia MyFCD + USDA SR Legacy component derivation
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia (Institute for Medical Research, MyFCD) and USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD braised chicken entries and USDA SR Legacy component data for fermented soybean paste, bamboo shoots, and palm sugar. No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Pongteh as a composite dish; values represent a weighted component derivation consistent with standard Nonya recipe proportions. Core macros cross-referenced against HPB Singapore food composition data for braised chicken dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meals and Analogous Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; chicken + fermented soybean sauce component estimation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Sydney University Glycemic Index Research Service, SUGiRS)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Pongteh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 (low) is an analogous estimate for the complete meal (chicken + sauce + 120g steamed white rice). The chicken and sauce component alone has an estimated GI of approximately 30 (low), dominated by protein and fat. The composite meal GI of 48 is derived by weighting the sauce/chicken component GI (~30) against steamed white rice GI (~72, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) at the proportions in the standard serving (300g chicken+sauce vs 120g rice). Glycemic load of 16.2 calculated as: (GI 48 × available carbohydrate per serving ~33.7g) / 100. This is a moderate GL for a complete meal.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from tau cheo (fermented soybean paste) — approximately 490mg per 100g; a 150g chicken-and-sauce serving delivers ~735mg sodium, and the complete meal with rice may approach 1,000mg sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Chicken Pongteh to occasional consumption (no more than once per week) and request reduced-sauce portions; home cooks should reduce tau cheo quantity and avoid adding additional soy sauce or salt to the dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Palm sugar (gula melaka, GI ~35) provides a lower glycaemic impact than white sugar; complete meal GL of ~16.2 is moderate; total sugars ~5.5g per 100g from palm sugar in sauce.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Pongteh is acceptable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes when served with a controlled rice portion (≤120g cooked rice); the palm sugar-based sauce is preferable to white-sugar equivalents, and the high protein content (14.5g/100g) supports glycaemic moderation — monitor total carbohydrate intake from the complete meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is relatively low at 1.8g per 100g (significantly lower than the pork belly version at ~5.5g/100g); sodium at 490mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; cholesterol 52mg/100g is moderate.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken Pongteh is a cardiovascularly preferable choice compared to Babi Pongteh due to substantially lower saturated fat; however, the high sodium content requires portion control and infrequent consumption for patients with established cardiovascular disease or those on sodium-restricted diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.8g/100g); beneficial micronutrients including zinc (1.8mg), selenium (18mcg), niacin (5.5mg), and vitamin B6 (0.3mg) from chicken; high sodium is the primary concern.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Pongteh is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis given its low saturated fat profile and beneficial B vitamins, zinc, and selenium from chicken; sodium intake should be monitored and the dish consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Pongteh (娘惹豆酱焖鸡, Ayam Pongteh) is a classic Peranakan / Nonya braised chicken dish originating from the Straits Chinese communities of Singapore and Malacca, Malaysia. It is the halal-compatible counterpart to Babi Pongteh, substituting chicken pieces (bone-in, skin-on) for pork belly while using the identical sweet-savoury sauce base of tau cheo (fermented soybean paste / 豆酱) and gula melaka (palm sugar). The dish is slow-braised until the chicken is tender and deeply infused with the dark, glossy sauce. Bamboo shoots and shiitake mushrooms are added for texture and umami depth. The flavour profile is intensely savoury with a distinct sweet-savoury balance from the palm sugar, and the fermented soybean paste provides a complex, slightly funky depth characteristic of Peranakan cooking. Compared to its pork counterpart, Chicken Pongteh is significantly lower in fat (6.5g vs ~15g per 100g) and saturated fat (1.8g vs ~5.5g per 100g), making it a more favourable clinical choice. Per 100g, it provides 148 kcal, 14.5g protein, 6.5g fat, 9.8g carbohydrate, and 490mg sodium. The high sodium from tau cheo is the primary clinical concern across hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of niacin (5.5mg), vitamin B6 (0.3mg), zinc (1.8mg), selenium (18mcg), and phosphorus (168mg) from the chicken. Halal status depends on restaurant certification — some traditional preparations use pork lard in the rempah base; always confirm halal certification when counselling Muslim patients. Served at Nonya restaurants across Singapore and at Peranakan festive dinners, it is typically plated with steamed white rice.
common_user_queries: chicken pongteh vs babi pongteh nutrition
Nonya chicken fermented bean paste nutrition

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chicken Pongteh as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD braised chicken entries and USDA SR Legacy component data for fermented soybean paste, bamboo shoots, and palm sugar, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore braised chicken data. The derivation is methodologically sound but not a direct measurement of the composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for steamed white rice and protein/fat-dominant sauces. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Pongteh as a composite braised dish is not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy at the composite-dish level as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite braised dish is not reported in the component sources used. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for chicken and plant-based sauce ingredients — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). Halal status: the dish is classified halal when prepared with chicken and without pork lard; restaurant and hawker versions should be individually verified for halal certification. The GI of 48 is an analogous estimate for the complete meal including 120g steamed white rice; the chicken-and-sauce component alone has an estimated GI of approximately 30. Glycemic load of 16.2 is calculated for the full 420g serving. The input description references 'potato stew' which appears to be an error — Chicken Pongteh does not traditionally contain potato; the correct ingredients are bamboo shoots and shiitake mushrooms, consistent with the detailed food_subgroup and llm_context in the input record. Q8 alias consistency self-check (iteration 3): all strings present in aliases_by_language verified verbatim in flat aliases array — english: Chicken Pongteh, Ayam Pongteh, ayam pongteh, Nonya braised chicken, Peranakan chicken fermented bean, tau cheo chicken, chicken pongteh, Nonya chicken braised in fermented soybean paste, Peranakan braised chicken, tau cheo ayam — all present; chinese: 娘惹豆酱焖鸡, 娘惹豆醬燜雞, 娘惹焖鸡 (Niángrě Mèn Jī) — all present; malay: Ayam Pongteh (Ayam Braised Taucho) — present; tamil: சிக்கன் போங்தே (Chicken Pongteh), சிக்கன் போங்தே — both present; hindi: चिकन पोंगतेह — present; romanized: Niángrě Dòujiàng Mèn Jī, Niángrě Mèn Jī — both present. All consistency checks passed.
